skill.name: concise-planning
skill.description: Generate clear, actionable, and atomic checklists for coding tasks with minimal back-and-forth.
skill.summary: Transform vague requests into structured development plans with defined scope, action items, and validation steps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 4 patterns that are all false positives. The external_commands detections at lines 18 and 37 are markdown formatting (inline code and code fences), not shell execution. The cryptographic algorithm detections at lines 3 and 67 are plain documentation text with no actual crypto operations. This skill is purely a planning template with no executable code, network access, or system interactions.

content.value_statement: Turn vague project requests into structured, executable checklists. Reduce back-and-forth by making reasonable assumptions and providing atomic, verb-first action items.
actual_capabilities: Analyze codebase context including README files and documentation
Generate plans with 6-10 atomic action items in logical order
Define clear scope boundaries with In/Out sections
Include validation and testing steps in every plan
Ask targeted questions only when truly blocking
Provide concrete file and module references in action items
limitations: Cannot execute code or run commands directly
Relies on available context and documentation quality
Does not estimate time or effort for tasks
Planning only - implementation requires separate execution

use_cases.title: Feature Development Planning
use_cases.description: Break down new feature requests into discrete, implementable steps with clear validation criteria.

use_cases.title: Refactoring Projects
use_cases.description: Create structured plans for codebase improvements with defined scope and rollback considerations.

use_cases.title: Bug Investigation
use_cases.description: Generate systematic debugging plans that cover discovery, hypothesis testing, and verification.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Feature Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a plan to add user authentication to our Express API. We use PostgreSQL and JWT. Include discovery, implementation, and testing steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starter template for straightforward feature requests

prompt_templates.title: Refactoring Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a refactoring of our monolithic UserService into separate modules for authentication, profile management, and notifications. Current file is src/services/user.ts (450 lines). Define clear scope boundaries and migration approach.

prompt_templates.title: Bug Investigation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Users report intermittent 500 errors on checkout. Create an investigation plan covering log analysis, reproduction steps, hypothesis testing, and monitoring setup. Payment provider is Stripe.

prompt_templates.title: Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan our migration from REST to GraphQL for the product catalog API. Include schema design, resolver implementation, gradual rollout strategy, and deprecation timeline for existing endpoints.

output_examples.input: Plan to add pagination to our product listing endpoint
output: # Plan
Implement offset-based pagination for the GET /products endpoint with configurable page size and sorting options.
## Scope
- In: Product listing endpoint, response format, query parameters
- Out: Other endpoints, caching layer, frontend changes
## Action Items
[ ] Review current GET /products implementation in src/routes/products.ts
[ ] Add query parameters: page, limit, sortBy, sortOrder
[ ] Implement offset calculation and database query modification
[ ] Add pagination metadata to response (total, page, totalPages)
[ ] Write unit tests for pagination logic
[ ] Add integration test verifying correct page contents
[ ] Update API documentation
[ ] Test with edge cases (empty results, last page, invalid params)
## Open Questions
- What is the default page size? (Assuming 20)
- Should we implement cursor-based pagination for large datasets?

output_examples.input: Plan to upgrade our Django project from version 3.2 to 4.2 LTS
output: # Plan
Execute a major version upgrade following Django's deprecation path with testing at each stage.
## Scope
- In: Django core, installed Django packages, test suite
- Out: Third-party services, deployment infrastructure, Python version
## Action Items
[ ] Audit installed packages for Django 4.x compatibility
[ ] Run tests on Django 3.2 to establish baseline
[ ] Upgrade to Django 4.0, fix deprecation warnings
[ ] Upgrade to Django 4.1, fix deprecation warnings
[ ] Upgrade to Django 4.2 LTS, verify all tests pass
[ ] Update requirements.txt and lock file
[ ] Review Django 4.2 release notes for security considerations
[ ] Deploy to staging environment for integration testing
## Open Questions
- Are there custom middleware or authentication backends that may be affected?
- What is the rollback plan if issues surface in production?

best_practices: Start with context gathering - read README, docs, and relevant code before planning
Keep action items atomic - each should be a single logical unit of work
Always include validation or testing as explicit steps in the plan
anti_patterns: Creating plans without understanding the existing codebase structure
Writing vague steps like 'fix the issue' instead of concrete actions
Skipping validation steps or assuming testing will happen separately

faq.question: When should I use this skill versus asking directly for code?
faq.answer: Use this skill when starting a complex task that benefits from structured thinking. It helps you see the full scope before implementation begins and ensures nothing is overlooked.

faq.question: How many action items should a plan include?
faq.answer: The skill generates 6-10 atomic items by default. This range balances completeness with cognitive load. Larger projects may need multiple plans at different abstraction levels.

faq.question: Will the skill ask me questions before generating a plan?
faq.answer: The skill asks at most 1-2 questions and only when truly blocking. It makes reasonable assumptions for non-critical unknowns to minimize back-and-forth.

faq.question: Can I modify the plan template structure?
faq.answer: The template provides a proven structure (Approach, Scope, Action Items, Validation). You can customize the sections in your prompt if your team has specific planning requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill work for non-coding tasks?
faq.answer: While designed for development work, the structured approach applies to any project needing atomic steps. The verb-first, concrete format translates well to other domains.

faq.question: What makes a good action item?
faq.answer: Good action items start with a verb (Add, Refactor, Verify), name specific files when possible, and represent a single logical unit of work that can be checked off when complete.
